summary: Jeff Atwood explores the evolution of physics-based gameplay, from classic vector arcade games to modern simulations powered by increasingly powerful hardware. He highlights the introduction of NVIDIA's GTX 280 with its 1.4 billion transistors, noting that GPUs now outperform CPUs for parallelizable tasks like video encoding by a factor of ten. The post surveys available physics engines for developers and recommends several physics-based games including Bridge Construction Set and Armadillo Run. Atwood argues that complex physics engines represent an excellent way to utilize the dormant computational power in modern PCs.
key_points: Modern GPUs contain more raw computational power than CPUs for highly parallelizable tasks, with the GTX 280 featuring 1.4 billion transistors
GPU-based video encoding can be ten times faster than even the fastest quad-core CPUs
Technologies like CUDA, Microsoft's Accelerator, and Apple's OpenCL will make GPU computing more accessible to developers
Multiple physics engines are available for programmers including Havok, Newton, ODE, and NVIDIA PhysX
Physics-based games like Bridge Construction Set demonstrate how simulation can make subjects like civil engineering entertaining
money_quotes: Accurately simulating the physics of the real world has been the domain of supercomputers for decades.
Now that we almost have multicore supercomputers on every desktop, it's only natural that aspect of computing would trickle down to us.
Some of the highest performing hardware in your PC lies on your video card.
Compared to the newest, speediest quad core CPU, you can encode video ten times faster using a modern video card GPU.
Who knew civil engineering could be so much fun?
After playing this game, you will learn to love the power and beauty of the simple triangle.
Increasingly complex physics engines are an outstanding way to harness the massive computational horsepower that lies dormant in most modern PCs.
key_insight: The massive parallel processing power of modern GPUs, previously reserved for graphics, is now accessible to developers through physics engines and general-purpose computing frameworks, opening new possibilities for simulation-based games and applications.
